---
title: "IV — Embedded systems"
description: "Software interacting with hardware, time, energy, communication, and failure."
page-role: part
part-id: embedded
---

An embedded system is correct only when its software, hardware, timing, power,
interfaces, recovery behavior, and update path work together. A function that
returns the right value too late—or only in the laboratory—is not enough.

## Part question

How does software control and observe a physical system predictably despite
concurrency, finite resources, imperfect communication, and faults?

## Entry knowledge

You should understand logic levels, state, timing, basic computer architecture,
and transistor-level interfaces. [T02](../appendices/t02-tools-version-control.qmd)
provides the version-control and automation practices assumed by code examples;
[T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd) supports reproducible
firmware releases and test records.

## Learning arc

| Chapters | Development |
|---|---|
| E01–E02 | connect C semantics and memory to registers, peripherals, and physical pins |
| E03 | reason about interrupts, deadlines, shared state, energy, and low-power modes |
| E04 | design robust local buses, network exchanges, framing, and fault handling |
| E05–E06 | scale to RTOS and Linux systems with dependability, security, services, and updates |

## Exit capability

You should be able to define a hardware/software interface, budget time and
energy, capture protocol evidence, reproduce a concurrency failure, and design a
bounded recovery or update mechanism. Tests should identify configuration and
expose failure paths rather than demonstrate only the nominal case.

Labs L11, L12, and L18 provide the principal driver, protocol, and recovery
evidence. Projects M2–M6 reuse these capabilities at increasing system scale;
see the [practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine).
